app.subtitle: Optimal lab scheduling with CP-SAT
res.empty: No equipment yet — add your first device.
res.availHint: Paint the slots when this unit is out of service (maintenance, calibration, booked elsewhere). The solver never assigns the unit during painted slots.
res.noUnits: Define equipment to manage unit availability.

eq.unitNamesTaken: A unit name collides with another equipment type.

import.invalid: Not a valid equipment pool JSON.
workhours.invalid: Work start must be before work end.
tasks.priorityHint: Drag rows to reorder — top = highest priority. Alt+↑/↓ also moves the selected task.
tasks.empty: No tasks yet — add your first task.
tasks.name: Task name
tasks.deadlineHint: task must finish by this time
tasks.earliestHint: task may not start before this time
tasks.pinnedHint: task starts exactly at this time
tasks.dependsHint: this task starts only after the selected tasks have ended
tasks.noDeps: No other tasks to depend on.
sch.pinned: Task pinned to its scheduled start.
sch.stale: Schedule is stale (horizon has moved) — re-solve.

ob.welcome.title: Welcome to LabPlanner
ob.welcome.body:
LabPlanner plans your lab work on shared equipment — optimally. You describe your devices and tasks; the Google OR-Tools CP-SAT solver finds the schedule that finishes everything as early as possible while respecting every constraint.

It runs entirely on your machine: every project is one local JSON file with automatic backups — no accounts, no cloud. Manage multiple projects (and undo any change with Ctrl+Z) from the header.
ob.resources.title: 1 · Define your resources
ob.resources.body:
Start in the Resources tab:

  • Add each equipment type and how many physical units you own (e.g. VSG ×3). Units can carry custom names.
  • Set your working hours and add public holidays — manually or auto-filled by country.
  • Paint maintenance windows on individual units; the solver never books them during those hours.
ob.tasks.title: 2 · Describe your tasks
ob.tasks.body:
In the Tasks tab, create a task for each piece of work:

  • Duration in half-hour steps, plus the equipment it needs.
  • Optional limits: work-hours-only, earliest start, deadline.
  • Paint time slots the task must avoid or should prefer.
  • Drag tasks in the list — higher means more important.
ob.schedule.title: 3 · Solve and share
ob.schedule.body:
Press Solve schedule. In seconds you get a provably optimal plan:

  • A per-unit Gantt chart — zoom in for hour lines, hover for details.
  • A table with start, end and deadline status for every task.
  • Export HTML creates a single-file report you can share.

Look for the ⓘ buttons around the app for detailed help on each section. Have fun!
info.title: About this section
info.solve.title: How the schedule is solved
info.solve.body:
LabPlanner computes a provably optimal schedule using Google OR-Tools CP-SAT. Time is divided into 30-minute slots over the rolling planning horizon (today plus the next 13 days).

Hard constraints — every schedule satisfies all of these:

  • Each task receives the requested quantity of every equipment type it needs.
  • No physical unit serves two tasks in the same time slot.
  • Units are never assigned inside their painted out-of-service windows.
  • Work-hours-only tasks run within working hours and skip weekends and public holidays; “continue on next day” lets them split across days.
  • Deadlines, earliest starts, pinned starts and slots painted unavailable are always honoured.
  • A task with dependencies starts only after all of its prerequisites have ended.
  • Done tasks are skipped; in-progress tasks stay frozen on their current units and times while the rest is re-planned.

Optimisation goals — applied in strict order of importance:

  1. Makespan: finish all tasks as early as possible.
  2. Preferred slots: place tasks on as many painted preferred slots as possible.
  3. Priority: start tasks nearer the top of the list earlier.

OPTIMAL means no better schedule exists. FEASIBLE means the time limit was reached and the best schedule found so far is shown. INFEASIBLE means no schedule can satisfy every constraint — the message usually includes a hint naming one constraint whose relaxation would make it feasible.
info.pool.title: Equipment pool
info.pool.body:
Define every equipment type your lab owns and how many physical units of it exist. Type name plus count expand into individual units (e.g. VSG ×3 → VSG-1…VSG-3), or give each unit its own name (serial number, brand) while editing the type.

  • Tasks request equipment by type; the solver picks which specific units to use.
  • Renaming a type or unit safely carries task references, availability windows and the solved schedule along.
  • Use the export/import buttons to share a pool between projects as JSON.
info.calendar.title: Working calendar
info.calendar.body:
The working calendar applies to tasks marked work hours only. Other tasks may run around the clock.

  • Work start/end are set in 30-minute steps and take effect immediately.
  • Public holidays are full days off, like weekends. Add dates manually or auto-fill a country's official holidays for a year.
  • Holidays inside the horizon are shaded in the grids and the Gantt, with the day name shown in red.
info.avail.title: Unit availability
info.avail.body:
Paint the time slots when a specific physical unit is out of service — maintenance, calibration, or booked outside LabPlanner.

  • Pick a unit, then click or drag over the grid to toggle slots.
  • The solver never assigns that unit to any task during painted slots; other units of the same type remain usable.
  • The eraser button clears every window of the selected unit.
info.tasks.title: Tasks
info.tasks.body:
Each task describes one piece of lab work. The list order sets priority: drag rows — the higher a task sits, the earlier the solver tries to start it.

  • Duration is set in half-hour steps.
  • Work hours only keeps the task inside the working calendar; enable continue on next day for tasks longer than one work day.
  • Earliest start and deadline bound when the task may run; a pinned start fixes it to an exact time.
  • Dependencies make the task wait for other tasks to finish.
  • Status drives re-planning: done tasks drop out of the schedule, in-progress tasks keep their place on re-solve.
  • Required resources request equipment by type and quantity (limited to what the pool holds).
  • In the slot grid, paint hours the task must avoid (unavailable) or should favour (preferred). Right-click paints preferred, middle-click clears.
info.schedule.title: Reading the schedule
info.schedule.body:
Each row is one physical unit; coloured blocks are task reservations. One task may occupy several rows at once.

  • Grey bands are off-hours, weekends and holidays; the red dashed line marks now.
  • Hover a block for full details; the table below lists start, end and deadline status per task.
  • Zoom with the magnifier buttons — hour and half-hour gridlines appear as you zoom in; unit names stay pinned while scrolling.
  • The pin button in the details table fixes a task to its scheduled start for the next re-solve.
  • The export menu downloads a self-contained interactive HTML report (with its own zoom), an Excel workbook of the table, or an ICS calendar you can import into Outlook/Google Calendar.
